prompts: Antibodies interact with antigens through specific binding sites known as paratopes that correspond to unique epitopes on antigens. This specificity ensures that antibodies target specific structures, leading to an immune response. The binding is highly selective, determined by the unique shapes and chemical properties of the epitopes and paratopes, analogous to a lock-and-key mechanism. Once bound, antibodies can neutralize antigens, mark them for destruction by other immune cells, or activate additional immune pathways, effectively contributing to a protective response against pathogens or foreign substances.
